tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1. Optimal for patients with solitary tumors, negative nodes, no extensive or multifocal CIS, no moderate/severe hydronephrosis, and good pre-treatment bladder function. [218-231]

tier_1_regimens.notes: May be curative in selected cases with solitary lesions <2 cm, minimal muscle invasion, no associated CIS, palpable mass, or hydronephrosis. [147,148,234]

tier_1_regimens.notes: Data support neoadjuvant chemotherapy for small cell bladder cancer. Alternatively, use regimens from NCCN Guidelines for Small Cell Lung Cancer. [317-321]

tier_2_handbook.definition: Bladder cancer is a malignancy arising from the urothelium that lines the urinary tract from the renal pelvis to the proximal two-thirds of the urethra. The bladder is the most common site, but urothelial carcinoma can also develop in the renal pelvis, ureters, and urethra. The 2022 World Health Organization (WHO) classification of tumors of the urinary system is used for grading and subtyping. The clinical spectrum is divided into three categories based on prognosis and management: non–muscle-invasive bladder cancer (NMIBC), muscle-invasive bladder cancer (MIBC), and metastatic disease. NMIBC includes tumors confined to the urothelium (Ta), carcinoma in situ (CIS), and tumors invading the lamina propria (T1). MIBC is defined by invasion into the muscularis propria (≥T2) and includes stages II through IV A. Metastatic disease (stage IVB) involves distant lymph nodes or visceral metastases. The depth of invasion is the most important determinant of prognosis and treatment. Although urothelial carcinoma is the predominant histology, bladder cancer encompasses a diverse group of histologic subtypes, including squamous cell carcinoma, adenocarcinoma, small cell carcinoma, and other rare variants, which may have different biological behaviors and treatment implications. The diagnosis is confirmed by transurethral resection of bladder tumor (TURBT) with pathological examination, and staging is determined using the American Joint Committee on Cancer (AJCC) TNM system, 8th edition. Risk stratification for NMIBC is based on the American Urological Association (AUA)/Society of Urologic Oncology (SUO) guidelines, incorporating factors such as tumor grade, size, multiplicity, and presence of CIS.

subtypes.name: Urothelial carcinoma
subtypes.description: The most common histology, arising from transitional epithelium. It includes conventional urothelial carcinoma and numerous invasive subtypes with divergent differentiation.
subtypes.frequency: Approximately 90% of bladder cancers in the United States and Europe.
subtypes.key_associations: May be associated with squamous or glandular differentiation. Aggressive subtypes include micropapillary, plasmacytoid, small cell/neuroendocrine, and sarcomatoid histologies, which are at higher risk for progression to muscle-invasive disease.

subtypes.name: Squamous cell carcinoma
subtypes.description: Composed exclusively of squamous cells with keratinization and/or intercellular bridges. Includes pure squamous cell carcinoma and verrucous carcinoma.
subtypes.frequency: Constitutes about 3% of urinary tumors in the United States. In regions endemic for Schistosoma haematobium, it may account for up to 75% of bladder cancers.
subtypes.key_associations: Associated with chronic irritation, infection (e.g., schistosomiasis), and pelvic radiation. Generally presents at an advanced stage.

subtypes.name: Adenocarcinoma
subtypes.description: Pure glandular phenotype, including enteric, mucinous, and mixed subtypes. Urachal carcinoma is a subtype arising from urachal remnants.
subtypes.key_associations: Urachal carcinomas involve the dome or anterior wall of the bladder. No proven role for neoadjuvant/adjuvant chemotherapy; treatment is primarily surgical.

subtypes.name: Small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma
subtypes.description: High-grade neuroendocrine tumor, often mixed with urothelial carcinoma.
subtypes.key_associations: Aggressive behavior; requires multimodal treatment including systemic therapy (similar to small cell lung cancer) and local therapy. May be associated with paraneoplastic syndromes.

subtypes.name: Other rare histologies
subtypes.description: Includes mesenchymal tumors (e.g., rhabdomyosarcoma, leiomyosarcoma), melanoma, carcinosarcoma, and tumors of Müllerian type (clear cell, endometrioid).
subtypes.frequency: Collectively account for <5% of bladder cancers.
subtypes.key_associations: Treatment varies by specific histology; some may require specific systemic therapies (e.g., sarcoma protocols).

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: Bladder cancer is characterized by a high mutational burden, with The Cancer Genome Atlas ranking it as the third most mutated cancer. Genomic profiling of advanced urothelial carcinoma reveals that 93% of cases harbor at least one clinically relevant genetic alteration, with a mean of 2.6 per case. The most commonly mutated genes include CDKN2A (in 34% of tumors), FGFR3 (21%), PIK3CA (20%), and ERBB2 (17%). FGFR3 alterations, including sequence variants, copy number changes, and rearrangements (particularly gene fusions), are therapeutically targetable with erdafitinib. HER2 overexpression, detected by immunohistochemistry (IHC), is a biomarker for fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki. Microsatellite instability (MSI) and/or mismatch repair (MMR) deficiency are evaluated to identify patients who may benefit from immune checkpoint inhibitors. Programmed death-ligand 1 (PD-L1) expression is a predictive biomarker for certain immunotherapies. Other recurrent genomic alterations involve chromatin remodeling genes (e.g., KMT2D, ARID1A), DNA damage response genes (e.g., RB1, TP53), and telomere maintenance genes (e.g., TERT promoter mutations). These molecular alterations drive key oncogenic pathways, including the fibroblast growth factor receptor (FGFR) pathway, the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway, and the receptor tyrosine kinase/RAS pathway, offering opportunities for targeted therapies.
tier_2_handbook.epidemiology: In the United States, an estimated 84,530 new cases of urinary bladder cancer (64,730 in males and 19,800 in females) were diagnosed in 2026, with approximately 17,870 deaths (12,640 in males and 5230 in females). Bladder cancer is the sixth most common cancer in the United States. The median age at diagnosis is 73 years, and it is rarely diagnosed in individuals under 40 years. There is a striking male predominance, with a male-to-female ratio of approximately 3:1. White individuals have a higher incidence compared to Black individuals, although Black patients often present with more advanced disease and have worse outcomes. Incidence rates have been declining over the past few decades, likely due to reductions in smoking prevalence. Globally, bladder cancer is the 10th most common cancer, with significant geographic variation; in regions endemic for Schistosoma haematobium, squamous cell carcinoma predominates. Upper tract urothelial carcinoma (UTUC), including tumors of the renal pelvis and ureter, is relatively uncommon, accounting for 5–10% of all urothelial carcinomas. Primary carcinoma of the urethra is rare, representing <1% of genitourinary malignancies. Urothelial carcinomas of the prostate and primary carcinoma of the urethra are distinct clinical entities with different staging systems and management approaches.

risk_factors.detail: The most established risk factor, accounting for approximately 50% of cases in men and 30% in women. Risk increases with duration and intensity of smoking and persists for years after cessation.
risk_factors.strength: Strong; cigarettes are the primary source, but pipe, cigar, and pipe smoking also increase risk.

risk_factors.detail: Exposure to aromatic amines (e.g., 2-naphthylamine, 4-aminobiphenyl), polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons, and chlorinated solvents (e.g., trichloroethylene) in industries such as dye, rubber, leather, painting, and metalworking.
risk_factors.strength: Moderate to strong; well-established in occupational settings.

risk_factors.detail: Prior radiation therapy for cancers of the cervix, prostate, or other pelvic malignancies increases the risk of bladder cancer, typically after a latent period of several years.
Chronic use of cyclophosphamide (an alkylating agent) and pioglitazone (a thiazolidinedione diabetes medication) has been associated with increased risk.

risk_factors.factor: Chronic urinary tract irritation or infection
risk_factors.detail: Long-term indwelling catheters, bladder stones, and chronic urinary tract infections are risk factors. Schistosoma haematobium infection is a major cause of squamous cell carcinoma in endemic regions.
risk_factors.strength: Moderate; particularly strong for squamous cell carcinoma in schistosomiasis-endemic areas.

risk_factors.factor: Personal or family history of bladder cancer
risk_factors.detail: A personal history of bladder cancer increases the risk of recurrence and new tumors. First-degree relatives of patients with bladder cancer have a modestly increased risk, suggesting a hereditary component.
risk_factors.strength: Moderate; family history may indicate shared genetic or environmental exposures.

risk_factors.detail: An autosomal dominant disorder caused by germline mutations in DNA mismatch repair genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2), leading to a significantly increased risk of urothelial carcinoma, particularly of the upper tract.
risk_factors.strength: Strong; lifetime risk of urothelial carcinoma is up to 8%.

risk_factors.detail: Men have a 3–4 fold higher incidence than women, which may be related to differences in smoking prevalence, occupational exposures, and hormonal factors.
White individuals have a higher incidence compared to Black individuals in the United States, although Black patients present with more advanced disease.
risk_factors.strength: Moderate; incidence differences may be influenced by socioeconomic and environmental factors.

risk_factors.detail: Obesity is associated with a modestly increased risk of bladder cancer, potentially mediated by chronic inflammation and hormonal dysregulation.
risk_factors.strength: Moderate; meta-analyses suggest a positive association.

risk_factors.detail: Type 2 diabetes mellitus is associated with an increased risk of bladder cancer, possibly due to hyperinsulinemia or medications used to treat diabetes.
risk_factors.strength: Moderate; evidence from meta-analyses is consistent.

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Bladder cancer typically presents with microscopic or gross hematuria, which is the most common initial symptom. Hematuria may be painless and intermittent, often leading to delayed diagnosis. Urinary frequency, urgency, and dysuria due to bladder irritation or reduced capacity are also common. A subset of patients may initially present with symptoms mimicking a urinary tract infection. In more advanced disease, upper tract obstruction or flank pain can occur due to tumor extension or ureteral involvement. The median age at diagnosis is 73 years, and the disease is rare in individuals under 40 years [2].

symptoms.name: Hematuria
symptoms.detail: Microscopic or gross painless hematuria; the most frequent presenting symptom.
symptoms.frequency: Most common; present in the majority of patients at diagnosis.
symptoms.alarm: Persistent or gross hematuria warrants urgent investigation.

symptoms.name: Urinary frequency/urgency
symptoms.detail: Symptoms of bladder irritation or reduced bladder capacity.
symptoms.alarm: Persistent symptoms without infection are concerning.

symptoms.name: Dysuria
symptoms.detail: Painful urination, often associated with infection or irritative symptoms.
symptoms.alarm: If persistent without evidence of infection, consider bladder pathology.

symptoms.name: Urinary tract infection symptoms
symptoms.detail: May mimic or coexist with bladder cancer, especially in older adults.
symptoms.frequency: Less common as an isolated presentation.
symptoms.alarm: Recurrent or refractory UTI-like symptoms should prompt cystoscopy.

symptoms.name: Obstructive symptoms
symptoms.detail: Flank pain, hydronephrosis, or renal failure due to ureteral obstruction by tumor.
symptoms.frequency: Less common; indicates advanced or obstructing tumor.
symptoms.alarm: Obstructive symptoms require urgent evaluation for metastatic or locally advanced disease.

signs.name: Palpable mass
signs.detail: A large pelvic mass may be felt on bimanual examination under anesthesia (EUA) in advanced disease.
signs.frequency: Uncommon at initial presentation; more indicative of T3/T4 disease.

signs.name: Hydronephrosis
signs.detail: May be detected on imaging as a sign of ureteral obstruction.

signs.name: Weight loss
signs.detail: Non-specific but may indicate advanced or metastatic disease.

atypical_or_missed: Asymptomatic microscopic hematuria may be dismissed as benign, delaying diagnosis.
Urinary symptoms attributed solely to benign prostatic hyperplasia or infection without cystoscopy.
Tumors in the bladder dome or anterior wall may be missed on initial cystoscopy.
Positive cytology with negative cystoscopy may lead to underdiagnosis of upper tract or prostatic urethral disease.

red_flags: Painless hematuria in an older adult (>40 years)
Persistent urinary symptoms (frequency, urgency, dysuria) without evidence of infection
Hydronephrosis or obstructive uropathy on imaging
Unexplained weight loss or bone pain suggesting metastatic disease
Positive urine cytology with negative cystoscopy (requires upper tract and prostatic urethral evaluation)

diagnostic.rationale: Direct visualization of the bladder urothelium to identify and characterize lesions.
diagnostic.key_findings: Papillary, sessile, or flat lesions; CIS; tumor location, size, and number.

diagnostic.rationale: Obtain tissue for histopathologic diagnosis and staging; includes muscle layer for depth assessment.
diagnostic.key_findings: Histologic type, grade, invasion depth (pT stage), lymphovascular invasion.

diagnostic.rationale: Detects exfoliated malignant cells; high specificity but lower sensitivity for low-grade tumors.
diagnostic.key_findings: Presence of malignant cells suggests high-grade or CIS.

diagnostic.test: Enhanced cystoscopy (blue light or narrow-band imaging)
diagnostic.rationale: Improves detection of flat lesions (CIS) and multifocal disease compared to white light cystoscopy.
diagnostic.key_findings: Increased detection of CIS and recurrent tumors; reduces recurrence rates when used with TURBT.

staging.test: CT urography (CTU) of abdomen/pelvis with excretory phase
staging.rationale: Evaluate upper urinary tracts for synchronous tumors and assess for local extension or metastases.
staging.indication: All patients with suspected or confirmed bladder cancer.

staging.rationale: Assess local tumor extent, especially for sessile or high-grade tumors; useful for T-staging and nodal evaluation.
staging.indication: Consider for locally advanced disease or when CT contraindicated.
All patients with muscle-invasive or metastatic disease; may be omitted in low-risk NMIBC.

staging.test: Bone scan or MRI of bone
staging.rationale: Detect bone metastases in symptomatic patients or those with elevated alkaline phosphatase.
staging.indication: If clinical suspicion or symptoms of bone metastases.

staging.rationale: May improve staging accuracy for muscle-invasive disease; useful in selected cases for detecting distant metastases.
staging.indication: Category 2B for staging of T2 or greater disease; not recommended for NMIBC.

biomarkers.rationale: Predictive for response to erdafitinib in metastatic urothelial carcinoma.
biomarkers.when: For patients with unresectable or metastatic disease considering targeted therapy.
biomarkers.mandatory: No, but recommended to guide therapy selection.

biomarkers.rationale: Predictive for response to fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan in HER2-positive tumors.
biomarkers.when: For patients with advanced disease considering antibody-drug conjugate therapy.
biomarkers.mandatory: No, but recommended for therapy selection.

biomarkers.test: Microsatellite instability (MSI) and mismatch repair (MMR) testing
biomarkers.rationale: Identify patients with high MSI or dMMR who may benefit from immunotherapy.
biomarkers.when: For all patients with advanced or metastatic disease.

biomarkers.rationale: May predict response to certain immune checkpoint inhibitors in first-line setting for cisplatin-ineligible patients.
biomarkers.when: For cisplatin-ineligible patients considering checkpoint inhibitor monotherapy.

biomarkers.rationale: Prognostic and predictive biomarker after cystectomy to guide adjuvant immunotherapy.
biomarkers.when: Post-cystectomy in patients not previously treated with checkpoint inhibitors.
biomarkers.mandatory: No, but recommended for risk stratification.

biomarkers.rationale: Alternative to cytology for detection of recurrence; higher sensitivity but lower specificity.
biomarkers.when: May be considered in surveillance of high-risk NMIBC.

do_not_miss: Adequate TURBT including muscle (muscularis propria) for accurate staging in high-grade disease.
Staging CTU to evaluate upper urinary tracts for synchronous tumors.
Cytology for high-grade tumors and CIS.
Bimanual EUA under anesthesia to assess local tumor fixation.
Consideration of variant histologies (e.g., micropapillary, plasmacytoid, small cell) which require aggressive management.
avoid: Routine mapping biopsies of normal-appearing urothelium (low yield, may cause injury).
Chest x-ray alone for staging of muscle-invasive disease (CT chest is preferred).
Use of carboplatin as a substitute for cisplatin in perioperative setting (no survival benefit).
FDG-PET/CT for staging of non-muscle-invasive bladder cancer.

staging.system: AJCC Cancer Staging Manual, 8th edition (2017) TNM system for bladder cancer [19].

t_stages.description: Tumor invades lamina propria (subepithelial connective tissue).
Tumor invades superficial muscularis propria (inner half).
Tumor invades deep muscularis propria (outer half).
Extravesical tumor directly invades adjacent structures.
Invades prostatic stroma, seminal vesicles, uterus, or vagina.
Invades pelvic wall or abdominal wall.

n_stages.description: Regional lymph nodes cannot be assessed.
Single regional lymph node metastasis in the true pelvis (perivesical, obturator, internal and external iliac, or sacral).
Multiple regional lymph node metastasis in the true pelvis.
Lymph node metastasis to the common iliac lymph nodes.

m_stages.description: Distant metastasis limited to lymph nodes beyond the common iliacs.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Noninvasive papillary carcinoma confined to the urothelium.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: Excellent (>90%); treatment intent: curative with intravesical therapy.
Excellent with treatment; risk of progression if untreated.
~80-90%; risk of progression depends on grade and other features.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative; TURBT ± intravesical therapy; consider cystectomy for high-risk features.
Curative; neoadjuvant chemotherapy followed by cystectomy or bladder-preserving chemoradiation.

stage_groups.criteria: T3a, T3b, T4a, or T1-T4a with N1, N0, M0
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Tumor extends into perivesical fat or involves prostate/uterus/vagina, or with single pelvic lymph node metastasis.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative intent with neoadjuvant chemotherapy, cystectomy, and/or chemoradiation.

stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Multiple regional or common iliac lymph node involvement.
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: ~15-25%; systemic therapy and consolidation local therapy.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative intent in selected cases with aggressive systemic therapy and local consolidation.

stage_groups.criteria: T4b, any N, M0; or any T, any N, M1a
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Locally advanced disease fixed to pelvic/abdominal wall or distant lymph node metastases beyond common iliacs.
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Palliative; systemic therapy, possible consolidative local therapy in responders.
Palliative; systemic therapy to prolong survival and maintain quality of life.

staging_pearls: Clinical stage (c) is based on EUA, TURBT, and imaging; pathologic stage (p) requires cystectomy and lymph node dissection.
Approximately 42% of patients are upstaged at cystectomy, highlighting the limitations of clinical staging.
pT1 sub-staging may have prognostic value but is not widely utilized.
Presence of lymphovascular invasion, variant histology, and prostatic urethral involvement are high-risk features.
The 8th edition subdivides stage III and IV for better prognostication.
For upper tract urothelial carcinoma, staging is similar but T3 definition differs (invades beyond muscularis into peripelvic fat or renal parenchyma for renal pelvis; periureteric fat for ureter).
Urethral carcinoma has a separate staging system based on anatomic site (prostatic, membranous, bulbous, pendulous).

management_principles.overview: Bladder cancer management is driven by three fundamental clinical categories: non-muscle-invasive disease (NMIBC), muscle-invasive non-metastatic disease (MIBC), and metastatic disease. The treatment philosophy differs fundamentally across these categories. For NMIBC, the primary goal is to reduce recurrences and prevent progression to a more advanced stage while minimizing treatment-related adverse events. For MIBC, a multidisciplinary approach combining systemic therapy, surgery, and/or radiation is required, with the goal of achieving cure. For metastatic disease, the objective is to prolong survival and maintain quality of life using agents with different mechanisms of action. Approximately 84,530 new cases of bladder cancer are diagnosed annually in the United States, with a median age at diagnosis of 73 years. Risk factors include smoking, personal or family history, pelvic radiation, environmental and occupational exposures, chronic urinary tract infection, male sex, white race, obesity, diabetes, and Lynch syndrome. The clinical spectrum ranges from indolent low-grade papillary lesions to aggressive muscle-invasive and metastatic disease [1,2,3,7].

by_intent.approach: TURBT with risk-stratified intravesical therapy or cystectomy for BCG-unresponsive disease; surveillance for recurrence

management_principles.mdt_requirements: A multidisciplinary team approach is essential for optimal bladder cancer management. The NCCN Panel includes medical oncologists, urologists, radiation oncologists, pathologists, radiologists, and patient advocates. MDT discussion is critical for treatment planning across all disease stages. For MIBC, bladder preservation with trimodal therapy (TURBT plus concurrent chemoradiotherapy) is endorsed by multiple international organizations including ICUD-EAU, NICE, and AUA/ASCO/ASTRO/SUO [210-212]. The optimal use of perioperative chemotherapy for muscle-invasive disease requires multidisciplinary input. Molecular and genomic testing for stages IVA/IVB should ideally be performed at diagnosis to facilitate treatment decision-making [250]. For patients with BCG-unresponsive disease, shared decision-making between patient and clinician regarding cystectomy versus bladder-sparing alternatives is essential.
management_principles.performance_status_guide: Performance status is a major determinant of treatment selection for metastatic disease and can often limit use of cisplatin-containing regimens. Patients eligible for cisplatin-based therapy include those with adequate renal function (GFR), acceptable performance status, and no significant hearing loss or neuropathy. For borderline GFR, timed urine collection may more accurately determine cisplatin eligibility [140]. Patients not eligible for cisplatin may receive carboplatin-based combinations in the metastatic setting. Regimens with lower toxicity profiles are recommended for patients with compromised liver or renal status or serious comorbid conditions. For NMIBC, the presence of comorbidities influences the choice between intravesical therapy and cystectomy. For patients with extensive comorbid disease or poor performance status who are not candidates for cystectomy or definitive chemoradiotherapy, treatment options include radiotherapy alone or TURBT alone.

Essential for diagnosis and staging; adequate resection with muscle in specimen; single-dose intravesical chemotherapy within 24 hours if non-muscle-invasive disease suspected [BL-1,BL-B]

items.text: Single-dose intravesical chemotherapy within 24 hours of TURBT
items.recommendation: Gemcitabine (category 1, preferred) or Mitomycin (category 1); reduces 5-year recurrence rate by approximately 35%; contraindicated if bladder perforation concern [BL-1,BL-F]
Early repeat TURBT (within 6 weeks) for incomplete initial resection, no muscle in specimen for high-grade disease, large ≥3 cm or multifocal lesions, or any T1 lesion [BL-B]

items.text: Ureteroscopy and biopsy or percutaneous biopsy and/or selective washings
items.recommendation: For upper tract evaluation and biopsy [UTT-1,UTT-3]
NOT recommended for most patients outside clinical trials; rarely yield positive results and lack sensitivity for CIS; often cause additional bladder damage without benefit [MS-4,15-18]
For suspected urothelial carcinoma of the prostate with ductal/acini or prostatic urethral involvement [UCP-1,BL-B]

items.text: Somatic molecular tumor testing for unresectable or metastatic disease
items.recommendation: Recommended at initial diagnosis and at recurrence if relevant; should cover FGFR3 alterations (sequence variants, copy number variants, rearrangements/gene fusions); larger MGPT may be preferred [BL-I]

items.text: Microsatellite instability (MSI) and/or mismatch repair (MMR) status evaluation
items.recommendation: Recommended for all patients with unresectable or metastatic bladder cancer [BL-I]
Recommended for all patients with unresectable or metastatic bladder cancer; gastric IHC scoring recommended based on DESTINY-PanTumor02 prospective data [BL-I,34]
Cover sequence variants, copy number variants, and rearrangements particularly gene fusions; for eligibility for erdafitinib [BL-I]
Consider for risk stratification and to determine use of adjuvant immunotherapy post-cystectomy; FDA-approved personalized tumor-informed multiplex PCR-NGS assay [BL-I,43]
SP142 assay for atezolizumab eligibility (PD-L1-stained tumor-infiltrating immune cells ≥5% of tumor area); for cisplatin-ineligible patients [BL-G]
Should be considered prior to fluoropyrimidine therapy [BL-G]
Required for all testing; only laboratories certified under CLIA-88 as qualified to perform highly complex molecular pathology testing [BL-I,250]
For TURBT, cystectomy planning, and all surgical management [BL-B]
For systemic therapy planning in neoadjuvant, adjuvant, and metastatic settings [BL-G]
For bladder preservation, adjuvant RT, and palliative RT [BL-H]
For confirming clinically significant rare subtype histology, confirming metastatic carcinoma of urothelial origin, and in clinically discrepant scenarios [BL-C]
For patients with suspected Lynch syndrome based on younger age at presentation and/or personal or family history of Lynch syndrome-related cancers [BL-1,7]

surgery.role: Surgical intervention is the primary treatment modality for most stages of bladder cancer. TURBT serves as both diagnostic and therapeutic for NMIBC and as a staging procedure for MIBC. Radical cystectomy with pelvic lymphadenectomy is the standard surgical treatment for MIBC stages II and IIIA. For BCG-unresponsive NMIBC, cystectomy is a preferred option. For UTUC, nephroureterectomy with bladder cuff is the standard surgical approach. Partial cystectomy is reserved for highly select patients with cT2 disease in favorable locations.
principles: Adequate TURBT with muscle in specimen is essential for accurate staging; muscle may be omitted only for documented low-grade Ta disease [BL-B]
Visually complete and safe TURBT is an essential part of bladder preservation and is associated with improved outcomes in non-metastatic settings [BL-B]
Enhanced cystoscopy (blue light and narrow-band imaging) may be helpful in identifying lesions not visible using white light [BL-B]
Repeat TURBT within 6 weeks recommended for incomplete initial resection, no muscle in specimen for high-grade disease, large/multifocal lesions, or T1 disease [BL-B]
Bilateral pelvic lymphadenectomy should be performed with radical cystectomy and partial cystectomy [BL-B]
A more extensive PLND yields more nodes, increases positive node yield, and may be associated with better survival and lower pelvic recurrence [156-160]
In appropriately selected patients, approaches preserving the uterus, vagina, fallopian tubes, and/or ovaries should be employed when feasible [149,150]
Cystectomy should be performed within 3 months of diagnosis if no therapy is given [BL-B]
Consultation/re-review from experienced GU pathologist as required for rare subtype histology confirmation [BL-C]

procedures.name: Transurethral Resection of Bladder Tumor (TURBT)
procedures.indications: All suspected bladder cancers for diagnosis, staging, and treatment of NMIBC; staging procedure for MIBC; therapeutic for select small solitary T2 lesions
procedures.key_complications: Bladder perforation (contraindication to immediate intravesical chemotherapy), bleeding, infection, urinary retention

procedures.name: Radical Cystectomy/Cystoprostatectomy
procedures.approach: Open or robotic (laparoscopic); includes prostatectomy in males (removal of prostate, seminal vesicles, proximal vas deferens, proximal urethra) and commonly hysterectomy in females (removal of uterus, ovaries, fallopian tubes, urethra, part of vagina)
procedures.indications: Primary treatment for cT2, cT3, cT4a MIBC; highly select cT4b responding to primary treatment; BCG-unresponsive high-risk NMIBC; residual high-grade cT1 with aggressive features; aggressive histopathologic subtypes; lymphovascular invasion; concomitant CIS
procedures.key_complications: Bleeding, infection, urinary diversion complications (ileal conduit or neobladder), incontinence (with orthotopic diversion), sexual dysfunction, lymphocele, ureteral stricture

procedures.name: Partial Cystectomy
procedures.approach: Open, laparoscopic, or robotic; includes bilateral pelvic lymphadenectomy
procedures.indications: Highly select cT2 disease with solitary lesion in location amenable to segmental resection with adequate margins; no CIS on random biopsies; cancer in bladder diverticulum; select UTUC cases
procedures.key_complications: Similar to radical cystectomy but with retained bladder function; risk of local recurrence; may require ureteral reimplantation
procedures.operative_mortality: Comparable to radical cystectomy in experienced centers

procedures.name: Radical Nephroureterectomy with Cuff of Bladder
procedures.indications: Primary treatment for non-metastatic high-grade upper GU tract tumors
procedures.key_complications: Bleeding, infection, urinary leak, loss of kidney function, bladder cuff recurrence

procedures.name: TUR of Prostate (TURP)
procedures.indications: Primary treatment for urothelial carcinoma of the prostate with ductal/acini or prostatic urethral pathology
procedures.key_complications: Bleeding, infection, incontinence, erectile dysfunction, TUR syndrome

procedures.name: Urethrectomy
procedures.approach: Distal or total; may include inguinal lymph node dissection in selected cases
procedures.indications: Primary treatment for CIS, Ta, T1 primary carcinoma of the urethra; patients with prior radical cystectomy and cutaneous diversion; T2 disease in selected cases
procedures.key_complications: Bleeding, infection, incontinence, urethral stricture, sexual dysfunction

procedures.name: Pelvic Exenteration (category 2B)
procedures.approach: Open; en bloc removal of urethra, bladder, and anterior vagina (female) or prostate (male); may include posterior vaginal wall and rectum for T4 tumors
procedures.indications: Therapy for recurrence in female patients with ≥T2 primary carcinoma of the urethra; highly locally advanced T4 urethral tumors
procedures.key_complications: Significant morbidity including permanent urinary and fecal diversion, sexual dysfunction, wound complications

surgery.resectability_criteria: Cystectomy is generally indicated for cT2, cT3, and cT4a disease. Highly select patients with cT4b disease that responds to primary treatment may be eligible for cystectomy. Partial cystectomy is reserved for highly select cT2 cases with solitary lesion in dome location amenable to segmental resection with adequate margins and no CIS on random biopsies. Patients with moderate/severe hydronephrosis are poor candidates for bladder-sparing procedures. TURBT alone may be curative for selected solitary lesions <2 cm with minimal muscle invasion, no CIS, no palpable mass, and no hydronephrosis. TURBT alone should not be considered for cT2-T4a disease except in the setting of definitive chemoradiotherapy as part of trimodal therapy.
surgery.pre_operative_optimisation: Adequate staging must be completed before surgery, including cross-sectional imaging (CTU or MRU), chest imaging, and laboratory studies. GFR must be assessed for cisplatin eligibility if neoadjuvant therapy is planned. Neoadjuvant cisplatin-based chemotherapy should be initiated as soon as possible, but not more than 8 weeks after diagnosis, to prevent upstaging after radical cystectomy [170]. If cisplatin-based neoadjuvant therapy is planned, timing should allow completion before surgery without compromising surgical outcomes. For patients with borderline renal function, timed urine collection may more accurately determine cisplatin eligibility [140]. Split-dose administration of cisplatin (eg, 35 mg/m² on days 1 and 2 or days 1 and 8) may be considered for borderline renal function (category 2B) [BL-G].

radiation_therapy.role: Radiation therapy plays multiple roles in bladder cancer management: (1) Bladder preservation with concurrent chemoradiotherapy as a category 1 alternative to radical cystectomy for MIBC stages II and IIIA in select patients; (2) Adjuvant RT (category 2B) for patients with pT3-4 disease with positive nodes or margins post-cystectomy; (3) Palliative RT for metastatic disease or recurrent pelvic tumor; (4) Definitive RT for urethral carcinoma. Radiotherapy alone is inferior to chemoradiotherapy and is not considered standard for patients who can tolerate combined therapy.
principles: Precede RT alone or concurrent chemoradiotherapy by visibly complete TUR of the tumor when safely possible [BL-H]
Simulating and treating patients with empty bladder preferred for daily reproducibility; bladder full for tumor boosts acceptable with image guidance [BL-H]
Use multiple fields from high-energy linear accelerator beams [BL-H]
Concurrent chemoradiotherapy is recommended for added tumor cytotoxicity and can be given without significantly increased toxicity over RT alone [BL-H,231]
Concurrent Fluorouracil and Mitomycin C or low-dose Gemcitabine can be used instead of cisplatin-containing regimens in patients with low or moderate renal function [BL-H]

Bladder preservation with concurrent chemoradiotherapy is most successful for patients without moderate/severe hydronephrosis and without extensive CIS associated with their muscle-invading tumor [BL-H,ff]

Optimal candidates for bladder preservation: solitary tumors, negative nodes, no extensive/multifocal CIS, no tumor-related moderate/severe hydronephrosis, good pre-treatment bladder function, <6 cm tumor, allows visually complete or maximally debulking TURBT [ff]

dose_frameworks.name: Whole bladder RT with or without pelvic nodal RT
dose_frameworks.indication: Initial treatment for bladder preservation; elective nodal treatment optional

dose_frameworks.name: Bladder tumor boost
dose_frameworks.indication: Boost to whole or partial bladder for invasive disease

dose_frameworks.name: Hypofractionated bladder RT
dose_frameworks.indication: Non-inferior to 64 Gy in 32 fractions; superior for invasive local control [232]

dose_frameworks.name: Adjuvant pelvic RT post-cystectomy
dose_frameworks.indication: For pT3/pT4 urothelial bladder cancer following radical cystectomy with ileal conduit (category 2B)

dose_frameworks.name: Boost for positive margins/extranodal extension post-cystectomy
dose_frameworks.indication: Boost for involved resection margins and areas of extranodal extension if feasible based on normal tissue constraints

dose_frameworks.name: Palliative bladder RT
dose_frameworks.indication: Palliative RT for metastatic or recurrent disease

approaches.name: Concurrent chemoradiotherapy with 5-FU/Mitomycin C
approaches.dose_fractionation: 5-FU + Mitomycin C concurrent with RT (category 1 for bladder preservation)
approaches.indication: Preferred radiosensitizer for bladder preservation; category 1 evidence from BC2001 trial: 2-year locoregional DFS 67% vs 54% with RT alone (P=.01); 5-year OS 48% vs 35% (P=.16) [37,231]
approaches.key_trial: BC2001 (James ND, et al. N Engl J Med 2012;366:1477-1488)
approaches.toxicities: Low rates of late grade ≥3 toxicities: 5.7% genitourinary, 1.9% gastrointestinal; no late grade 4 toxicities or treatment-related deaths [230]

approaches.name: Concurrent chemoradiotherapy with low-dose Gemcitabine
approaches.dose_fractionation: Low-dose gemcitabine concurrent with RT (preferred regimen)
approaches.indication: Preferred radiosensitizer; especially useful for patients with low or moderate renal function

approaches.name: Concurrent chemoradiotherapy with Cisplatin/Fluorouracil
approaches.dose_fractionation: Cisplatin + 5-FU concurrent with RT (category 2B for organ preservation)

approaches.name: Concurrent chemoradiotherapy with Cisplatin/Paclitaxel
approaches.dose_fractionation: Cisplatin + Paclitaxel concurrent with RT (category 2B for organ preservation)

approaches.name: RT alone
approaches.dose_fractionation: 55-64 Gy in conventional or hypofractionated schedule
approaches.indication: For patients who cannot tolerate concurrent chemotherapy; inferior to chemoradiotherapy [231,233]
approaches.toxicities: Lower acute toxicity than chemoradiotherapy but inferior outcomes

approaches.name: Postoperative adjuvant pelvic RT for UTUC
approaches.indication: Not routinely recommended based on limited data; one study showed reduced local recurrence (HR 0.177; P=.001) but no survival benefit [345,346]
approaches.key_trial: Li X et al. World J Surg Oncol 2020;18:114

systemic_therapy.overview: Systemic therapy plays critical roles across the spectrum of bladder cancer. In the perioperative setting, cisplatin-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy before radical cystectomy is a category 1 recommendation for MIBC, supported by meta-analyses showing improved 5-year OS and DFS. Adjuvant systemic therapy options include cisplatin-based chemotherapy, checkpoint inhibitors (nivolumab, pembrolizumab), and ctDNA-MRD-guided atezolizumab. For metastatic disease, the treatment landscape has evolved significantly with the emergence of immune checkpoint inhibitors, antibody-drug conjugates (enfortumab vedotin, sacituzumab govitecan, fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan), and targeted therapy (erdafitinib). The preferred first-line regimen for metastatic disease is enfortumab vedotin + pembrolizumab based on the EV-302 trial. Carboplatin should not be substituted for cisplatin in the perioperative setting [BL-G].

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: DDMVAC with growth factor support for 3-6 cycles
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Category 1 evidence; meta-analysis of 11 trials: 5% absolute OS improvement and 9% DFS improvement with cisplatin-based neoadjuvant chemotherapy [1,2,9,10,163,167]; VESPER trial: ddMVAC showed superior organ-confined response (77% vs 63%; P=.001) and 3-year PFS (66% vs 56%; HR 0.70; P=.025) vs GC [183]

by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Cisplatin/Gemcitabine + Durvalumab for 4 cycles prior to cystectomy, then Durvalumab for 8 cycles after cystectomy (category 1, bladder cancer only)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Based on NIAGARA trial [5]; Enfortumab vedotin-ejfv + Pembrolizumab for cisplatin-ineligible patients based on KEYNOTE-905 [6]
alternatives: Enfortumab vedotin-ejfv + Pembrolizumab preoperatively, then postoperatively (for cisplatin-ineligible) [6]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Adjuvant therapy — No prior platinum neoadjuvant (pT3, pT4a, pN+)
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: DDMVAC with growth factor support for 3-6 cycles
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Meta-analysis shows 25% mortality reduction with adjuvant chemotherapy [10,188]; VESPER: ddMVAC showed improved 3-year PFS (64% vs 56%; HR 0.77; P=.066) and time to progression (69% vs 58%; HR 0.68; P=.014) vs GC [183]
alternatives: Nivolumab (category 1) or Pembrolizumab; Atezolizumab if ctDNA-MRD positive within 1 year post-cystectomy (category 1) [7,43]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Adjuvant therapy — Prior platinum neoadjuvant (ypT2-ypT4a or ypN+)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: CheckMate 274 trial: DFS 20.8 vs 10.8 months (HR 0.70; P<.001) in ITT population; benefit most appropriate for patients who value opportunity to delay recurrence even if chance of cure not improved [7,195]

alternatives: Cisplatin/Gemcitabine (category 1) + Avelumab maintenance; Cisplatin/Gemcitabine + Nivolumab (category 1) + Nivolumab maintenance; DDMVAC (category 1) + Avelumab maintenance [15,16,261,282]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-line metastatic disease — Cisplatin-eligible, platinum-based approach
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: JAVELIN Bladder 100: OS 21.4 vs 14.3 months (HR 0.69; P=.001) [282]
alternatives: Cisplatin/Gemcitabine + Nivolumab followed by Nivolumab maintenance (CheckMate 901: OS 21.7 vs 18.9; HR 0.78; P=.02) [261]; DDMVAC + Avelumab maintenance [15,282]

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: Preferred regardless of cisplatin eligibility based on EV-302 [260]

by_stage_and_setting.setting: First-line metastatic disease — Not eligible for any platinum-containing chemotherapy
alternatives: Pembrolizumab monotherapy (only if not eligible for any platinum); Atezolizumab (only if PD-L1 positive or not eligible for any platinum; category 2B) [18,19,262,268]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: PD-L1 testing (SP142 assay, ≥5% tumor-infiltrating immune cells) for atezolizumab; PD-L1 CPS ≥10 no longer required for pembrolizumab first-line

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Second-line metastatic disease — Post-platinum, no prior immunotherapy or EV
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Biomarker-directed therapy (erdafitinib if FGFR3 alterations); Enfortumab vedotin-ejfv + Pembrolizumab
alternatives: Pembrolizumab (category 1 post-platinum: KEYNOTE-045 OS 10.3 vs 7.4; P=.002 [284]); Enfortumab vedotin-ejfv; Avelumab (category 2B); Nivolumab (category 2B) [23,297]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: FGFR3 genomic alterations for erdafitinib eligibility; HER2 expression for fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan eligibility

by_stage_and_setting.setting: Subsequent-line metastatic disease — Post-platinum and post-checkpoint inhibitor, no prior EV
by_stage_and_setting.preferred_regimen: Enfortumab vedotin-ejfv (category 1); Biomarker-directed therapy (erdafitinib if FGFR3 alterations, category 1)
by_stage_and_setting.rationale: EV-301: OS 12.88 vs 8.97 months (HR 0.70; P=.001) [297]; THOR erdafitinib: OS 12.1 vs 7.8 months (HR 0.64; P=.005) [293]
alternatives: Cisplatin/Gemcitabine; DDMVAC; Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki (if HER2 IHC 3+ or IHC 2+ with no alternatives [34,303]); Sacituzumab govitecan (useful in certain circumstances) [32]
by_stage_and_setting.biomarker_requirements: FGFR3 alterations for erdafitinib; HER2 expression for fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan

by_stage_and_setting.rationale: KEYNOTE-057: 12-month CR rate 19% for CIS; caution due to systemic toxicities [109]

key_regimens.name: DDMVAC (Dose-Dense MVAC)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression (neutropenia), mucositis, nausea/vomiting, nephrotoxicity, alopecia; 24.6% alive at 5 years in metastatic setting vs 13.2% for standard MVAC [171]

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Gemcitabine (GC)
key_regimens.cycle_days: 21-day cycle preferred; better dose adherence with fewer delays
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Myelosuppression, nausea/vomiting, nephrotoxicity, fatigue; 1% toxic deaths vs 3% for MVAC [198,255,256]

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Gemcitabine + Durvalumab (Perioperative)
key_regimens.setting: Perioperative/sandwich therapy for bladder cancer MIBC
key_regimens.cycle_days: 4 cycles preoperatively, then Durvalumab 1120 mg IV day 1 for 8 cycles postoperatively (21-day cycles)
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Immune-related adverse events, myelosuppression, nausea; based on NIAGARA trial [5]

key_regimens.name: Enfortumab vedotin-ejfv + Pembrolizumab (First-line metastatic)
key_regimens.setting: First-line locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Peripheral sensory neuropathy (55.6%), fatigue (51.1%), alopecia (48.9%); grade ≥3 treatment-related AEs in 55.9% [258-260]

key_regimens.name: Cisplatin/Gemcitabine + Nivolumab followed by Nivolumab maintenance
key_regimens.setting: First-line locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma

drugs.name: Nivolumab maintenance
drugs.day: every 2 weeks for up to 2 years

key_regimens.cycle_days: 21-day cycle for 4 cycles, then nivolumab maintenance
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade ≥3 AEs 61.8% (vs 51.7% chemotherapy alone); ORR 57.6% (vs 43.1%); CR 21.7% [261]

key_regimens.name: Enfortumab vedotin-ejfv monotherapy
key_regimens.setting: Post-platinum, post-checkpoint inhibitor metastatic urothelial carcinoma
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia (35%), leukopenia (18%), anemia (14%), diarrhea (10%), febrile neutropenia (10%); OS 12.88 vs 8.97 months (HR 0.70; P=.001) [296,297]

key_regimens.name: Erdafitinib
key_regimens.setting: Subsequent-line metastatic urothelial carcinoma with FGFR3 genomic alterations
key_regimens.cycle_days: 28-day cycle; dose may be increased to 9 mg based on tolerability and response
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade ≥3 treatment-related AEs 45.9%; hyperphosphatemia, stomatitis, diarrhea; OS 12.1 vs 7.8 months (HR 0.64; P=.005) in THOR cohort 1 [291,293]

key_regimens.name: Pembrolizumab monotherapy (Post-platinum)
key_regimens.setting: Second-line locally advanced or metastatic urothelial carcinoma post-platinum
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade ≥3 treatment-related AEs 15.0% (vs 49.4% chemotherapy); OS 10.3 vs 7.4 months (P=.002) [23,284]

key_regimens.name: Avelumab maintenance
key_regimens.setting: Maintenance after first-line platinum-based chemotherapy with no progression
key_regimens.cycle_days: 21-day cycle until disease progression or unacceptable toxicity
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade ≥3 AEs 47.4% vs 25.2% BSC alone; OS 21.4 vs 14.3 months (HR 0.69; P=.001) [15,282]

key_regimens.name: Nivolumab adjuvant
key_regimens.setting: Adjuvant therapy for MIBC post-cystectomy with ypT2-ypT4a/ypN+ after neoadjuvant chemotherapy
key_regimens.cycle_days: 21-day or 28-day cycle for up to 12 months
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade ≥3 treatment-related AEs 17.9% vs 7.2% placebo; DFS 20.8 vs 10.8 months (HR 0.70; P<.001) [7]

key_regimens.name: BCG (Intravesical)

drugs.name: BCG
drugs.day: weekly x 6 weeks induction, then maintenance at months 3, 6, 12, 18, 24, 30, 36

key_regimens.cycle_days: 6-week induction, then 3-week maintenance cycles (SWOG regimen); ideal maintenance 1 year for intermediate-risk, 3 years for high-risk
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Dysuria (60%), flu-like symptoms, urinary frequency/urgency; BCG sepsis (rare but serious); no increase in toxicity with cumulative doses; dose reduction encouraged if substantial local symptoms during maintenance [79-83]

key_regimens.name: Fam-trastuzumab deruxtecan-nxki (T-DXd)
key_regimens.setting: Subsequent-line metastatic urothelial carcinoma with HER2-positive (IHC 3+ or IHC 2+) expression
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade ≥3 drug-related AEs 40.8%; adjudicated drug-related interstitial lung disease 10.5% (1.1% fatal); ORR 39.0% for bladder cancer cohort; median PFS 7.0 months [34,303]

key_regimens.name: Sacituzumab govitecan-hziy
key_regimens.setting: Subsequent-line metastatic urothelial carcinoma post-platinum and post-checkpoint inhibitor
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Neutropenia (35%), leukopenia (18%), anemia (14%), diarrhea (10%), febrile neutropenia (10%); ORR 27%; median DOR 7.2 months [32,301]

key_regimens.name: Nadofaragene firadenovec-vncg (Intravesical)
key_regimens.cycle_days: Every 3 months for up to 4 instillations
key_regimens.key_toxicities: Instillation site discharge (24.3%), fatigue (23.4%), bladder spasm (17.8%), urinary urgency (16.8%); 12-month CR rate 24.3%; 24-month cystectomy-free survival 64.6% [104,105]

key_regimens.name: Gemcitabine intravesical system (TAR-200)
key_regimens.setting: BCG-unresponsive high-risk NMIBC with CIS ± papillary tumors

drugs.name: Gemcitabine intravesical system (TAR-200)
drugs.day: Every 3 weeks for up to 6 cycles

key_regimens.key_toxicities: Grade ≥3 AEs 12.9%; urinary tract pain most common; ORR 82.4% for CIS cohort; median DOR 25.8 months [103]

treatment_response_assessment.title: Treatment Response Assessment in Bladder Cancer
treatment_response_assessment.timing: Response assessment timing varies by disease context: (1) For MIBC receiving neoadjuvant therapy: reassess after completion of neoadjuvant cycles before cystectomy; (2) For stage IIIB receiving downstaging systemic therapy: reassess after 2-3 cycles; (3) For bladder preservation with chemoradiotherapy: reassess 2-3 months after completion; (4) For metastatic disease: reassess after 2-3 cycles of first-line chemotherapy, then after every 2 cycles; (5) For post-cystectomy surveillance: routine follow-up per risk-stratified tables
response_logic: For MIBC receiving neoadjuvant chemotherapy: Pathologic response at cystectomy is the primary measure; ypT0N0 indicates complete pathologic response; ypT2-ypT4a/ypN+ indicates residual disease requiring adjuvant therapy consideration

For stage IIIB receiving downstaging systemic therapy: Complete response (no tumor on imaging and cystoscopy/EUA) may be followed by observation or consolidation local therapy; Partial response (disease confined to bladder) may receive cystectomy or chemoradiotherapy; Progression is treated as metastatic disease

For bladder preservation (chemoradiotherapy): Reassess 2-3 months after completion with cystoscopy, EUA, TURBT, and imaging; if no residual tumor, surveillance; if persistent T2+, consider cystectomy; if CIS/Ta/T1, consider TURBT ± intravesical therapy or treat as metastatic disease if not suitable

For metastatic disease: Reassess after 2-3 cycles; continue for 2 more cycles if response or stable disease; maximum 6 cycles in most cases; if no response after 2 cycles, change therapy considering current performance status, disease extent, and prior therapy

ctDNA-MRD testing: May be considered within 1 year post-cystectomy for risk stratification and to guide adjuvant immunotherapy (atezolizumab) using FDA-approved personalized tumor-informed multiplex PCR-NGS assay [43]
imaging_recommendations: CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast is standard for response assessment [BL-A]
For NMIBC: Upper tract imaging (CTU or MRU) at baseline; for high-risk also at 12 months and every 1-2 years up to 10 years [BL-A]
For MIBC: Chest CT preferred for staging; CTU or MRU for abdomen/pelvis; consider FDG-PET/CT (category 2B) if not previously done or metastatic disease suspected [BL-A]
For metastatic disease: CT chest/abdomen/pelvis every 1-3 months during treatment; consider FDG-PET/CT if metastatic disease suspected [BL-E,BL-A]

Imaging with CT of chest/abdomen/pelvis with contrast for reassessment after primary treatment of MIBC; if no evidence of distant disease, further cystoscopic assessment may be considered [BL-7,footnote hh]
Bone imaging (MRI, FDG-PET/CT [category 2B], or bone scan) only if clinical suspicion or symptoms of bone metastases [BL-A]
biopsy_or_salvage_logic: TURBT should be performed for assessment after chemoradiotherapy to confirm complete response (cT0 = pT0 achieved in all patients with complete response after chemoradiation in one series [218])
For positive cystoscopy during surveillance: Repeat TURBT to reclassify AUA/SUO risk group and treat accordingly [BL-5,BL-11]

For persistent positive cytology with negative cystoscopy/imaging: Consider repeat cytology in 3 months, selected mapping biopsies including TURP, upper tract evaluation with ureteroscopy, enhanced cystoscopy, and evaluation of contiguous organs (vagina, cervix, rectum) via specialist referral [BL-5,136-139]

For suspected recurrence after bladder preservation: Treat as new cancer; CIS/Ta/T1 managed with intravesical therapy, cystectomy, or TURBT; invasive disease managed with radical cystectomy (second attempt at bladder preservation not advisable) [MS-28]
For post-cystectomy recurrence: Local recurrence treated as described; systemic therapy for metastatic recurrence; consider palliative RT for selected cases [BL-11,MS-28]
